data.title: Waste Management: Turning Trash To Cash
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman.

Waste Management, Inc. (NYSE:WM) is the largest waste management company in the U.S. and one of the largest in the world, without having significant exposure outside of North America. This is almost a utility-like business, as cash flows can be quite predictable, as trash removal is something nearly everyone needs this service. People and companies can do it themselves, but it often isn't convenient or even cost-effective; WM streamlines the process and can be competitive, given its significant size.

The company has an optimistic outlook for earnings and revenue to continue to grow, helped partially by its announced acquisition of Stericycle (SRCL) expected to close sometime this quarter. With strong earnings growth, this company can continue to keep up its dividend growth streak. Ultimately, this company turns trash (and recycling) into cash for its shareholders. We will learn more when the company reports Q3 earnings on Monday, October 28.

Earnings Review and Outlook

In the company's last earnings report for Q2 2024, they delivered a fairly rare miss on both the top and bottom lines. For both revenue and EPS estimates, that was only the fifth time in the last 16 quarters the company had missed expectations.

By far the largest business segment for the company is the collection and disposal, with recycling processing and sales a very distant but growing second. They also have a growing "WM Renewable Energy" segment.

The renewable energy business is through landfill gas-to-energy facilities; as trash decomposes, it releases methane gas that can be collected. Turning something that is literally waste into something useful to help power their natural gas fleet of vehicles. Further, it is expected to turn into additional tax credits as well.

The IRS issued proposed regulations applicable to the investment tax credits that could call into question our ability to realize some, or all, of this tax benefit, which would negatively impact financial expectations in connection with our significant planned and ongoing investments in sustainability growth projects in our WM Renewable Energy segment. In coordination with other members of the RNG industry and external advisors we are engaging directly with the U.S. Congress, the current federal administration, and other biogas sector stakeholders to encourage the Treasury Department to further refine its analysis prior to publication of final regulations that more accurately reflect the express language and legislative intent of the statute with respect to the investment tax credit. However, there is no guarantee that such efforts will be successful. We expect that the production tax credit incentives for investments in renewable energy and carbon capture, as expanded by the IRA, will likely result in an incremental benefit to the Company, although at this time, the anticipated amount of such benefit has not been quantified.

The North American waste management market is expected to be about $200 billion this year and grow around 4% annually. However, both earnings and revenue estimates show that WM is expected to grow even faster than that. In terms of revenue growth, it is a more modest ~6.3% average over the next five years.

On the other hand, EPS is expected to grow at nearly 11% annually over the coming five years. In particular, analysts are quite optimistic this year that WM can achieve some hefty growth. Then this moderates in the next two years but remains quite healthy at a just over double-digit pace before moderating a bit more after that. However, the further out we go, the fewer analysts who provide numbers, meaning the consensus can swing quite wildly.

While the company missed its expectations last quarter for earnings, there was a huge upside surprise in Q1 for the company. In the first half of the year, they've been able to deliver $3.57, leaving what would appear to be a realistic $3.70 they need to hit the estimates in the back half.

Helping to achieve that difference will be cost savings and also share buybacks. Along with the impressive dividend streak over the last 20 years, the company has been able to reduce its share count through repurchases. Over the last decade, that's been a trend outside of COVID-19 when they started to pare back a bit, and outstanding shares stayed about flat.

As we can see, though, even during Covid, the company's earnings did not take too much of a hit. This is thanks to its relatively stable business as an almost utility-like company. Then, the recovery post-Covid was swift, with 2021 being another strong year, pushing to even higher EPS than pre-Covid.

Further helping to drive potential growth for the company is the acquisition that is planned to happen this quarter of SRCL.

Stericycle has a leading position in the growing medical waste industry. The planned acquisition adds complementary business platforms to further our leading suite of comprehensive waste and environmental solutions. And these strategic benefits are accompanied by attractive financial benefits. Our team is progressing through the regulatory approval process and integration planning, and we're excited to welcome Stericycle's team members to WM.

They've highlighted that it is expected to be accretive to "WM's operating EBITDA and cash flows within one year of close." The acquisition is being funded through additional debt, and it'll push their leverage ratio to around 3.6x. They noted that they would expect to see their leverage ratio come back down to 2.75x to 3x in around 2 years. That's why share buybacks are expected to stop temporarily. Once they hit their leverage target, they'll resume repurchases, assuming all goes well.

In light of this elevated leverage, we're temporarily suspending our share repurchase program so that we can work our way back through our targeted leverage range of 2.75 times to 3 times, about 24 months after the close of Stericycle. The slight revisions in our projected leverage figures since our announcement of the planned acquisition of Stericycle are updates that reflect the impact of layering on the additional Tuck-In acquisition activity this year.

That's a fairly quick turnaround time to start seeing results, and if the acquisition closes as expected, we could start to see the benefits as soon as next year. However, the latest hold up is by Canadian regulators, as they've extended their review of the acquisition.

I think this is generally a favorable move; even though it will see share repurchases halted for a period and added leverage, it's focusing on growing the business. That can be considered a mostly more favorable move than share repurchases anyway; if they can do it successfully as a complementary business such as SRCL would seem to be an easy fit.

I find it particularly interesting that it will help to also provide a bit of a toe hold into new European markets. This is also providing some new exposure to customers in North America to help grow market share in their home markets as well.

Falling Into The Fair Value Range

The stock has been on quite a solid run this year, as the share price has lifted just over 16% through 2024. However, it was primarily the first quarter of this year and a significant pop early on that helped to lift the shares to where they are today. Since then, shares have been moving sideways. That said, that's not necessarily a negative, either.

That has helped to get the shares back into the fair value range based on its historical earnings multiple. Shares of WM are currently trading at a rather rich 28.6x forward P/E. With the S&P 500 Index (SP500) trading at what is considered a historically rich valuation and WM being even higher than that, it could be a concern for investors.

That said, on a relative basis to its historical range, that isn't all that rich at all. In fact, with the shares moving only sideways through most of this year and seeing earnings continue to grow, the shares have fallen back into their historical fair value range.

Investors with a long-term outlook of expecting to hold for years to come may not be so picky. However, more patient investors can try to wait until it falls below its fair value range, with a share price of ~$177. Alternatively, using a dollar-cost average approach is always an option for investors as well.

For what it is worth, analysts have an average price target of $222.50-suggesting that there is some upside to be had. Unfortunately, unlike the cold, hard data based on relative historical valuations, analysts tend to just adjust their price targets based on where shares are currently trading. If shares are rising, analysts will raise their price target and vice versa.

Getting Paid To Wait

One of the reasons to initiate a position now or through a DCA approach would be to start participating in receiving the cash this company spins out through its dividend every quarter. The company has delivered consistent dividend growth for 20 years now and at a fairly healthy pace. Over the last 5 years, the increases have averaged around 8%.

Given the expectation for continued earnings growth and a modest payout ratio of around 47.6% over the last year, that looks set to continue. Based on forward estimates, the payout ratio would come in at ~41.3%.

When looking at FCF per share, it comes out to a payout ratio of 57%-a bit more elevated, relatively speaking, but nothing to be overly concerned about either. Looking at it another way, they are expected FCF to hit $2 to $2.15 billion this year while expecting to pay just over $1.203 billion in dividends based on the current quarterly rate.

Conclusion

WM provides investors with a fairly steady business and has been able to deliver some pretty stellar earnings growth as well. With a planned acquisition, the company is set to grow further into a new complementary business. This will increase their leverage, which will put a pause on share repurchases. However, investing in the business for added growth should be a priority over buybacks anyway.

Waste Management, Inc. shares are looking richly priced on an absolute basis-but on a relative basis, that changes the picture to show that it's actually a better value now than it has been most of the last year. A more patient investor could wait for a more opportune entry price or utilize a dollar-cost average approach. An investor looking to hold for 3+ years probably doesn't need to be so picky anyway, assuming they can continue to grow earnings as expected.

data.title: PFLD: Hybrid Assets ETF With Low Rate Sensitivity, But Underwhelming Record
data.description:
PFLD strategy

AAM Low Duration Preferred and Income Securities ETF (NYSEARCA:PFLD) started investing operations on 11/19/2019 and tracks the ICE 0-5 Year Duration Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index. PFLD has 209 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 6.44% and a total expense ratio of 0.46%. It pays monthly distributions. Preferred stocks are considered hybrid assets because their behavior is between stocks and bonds. They have priority over common stocks in the payment of dividends and proceeds of liquidation, but are generally junior to debt and do not carry voting rights. Convertible bonds are also hybrid assets.

As described in the prospectus by Advisors Asset Management, the Underlying Index includes dollar-denominated, U.S. listed preferred and hybrid securities “with an option-adjusted duration of less than five years”. PFLD is designed to limit volatility related to interest rates, as a lower duration means less sensitivity to yield variations. Eligible securities must meet some requirements, in particular regarding amount outstanding and liquidity. They must be listed in the U.S., but their issuers may be foreign companies. The index is capitalization-weighted and rebalanced on a monthly basis, with a maximum allocation of 4.75% to any issuer (a company may have several securities in the index). The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 100%.

Portfolio

The top 10 issuers, listed in next table, represent 33.9% of asset value. The portfolio is quite diversified and risks related to individual companies are moderate.

However, the portfolio is overweight in the financial sector. The banking industry represents 24.7% of asset value, while REITs are at 17.5% (mostly mortgage REITs), financial services also at 17.5%, insurance at 15.9% and utilities at 10%. Other industries are below 4%.

Performance

Since inception in November 2019, PFLD share has lost 15% in value, as reported on the next chart. The cumulative inflation was about 23% in the same time, based on the Consumer Price Index. It represents a 34% loss in inflation-adjusted value for shareholders.

The annual sum of distributions increased from $1.20 per share in 2020 to $1.48 in 2023. This is a 23% dividend growth, just offsetting inflation.

However, PFLD history is short. ETFs in the same asset category with a longer track record like Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX), reviewed in June, show a decay in both value and distribution in the long term.

PFLD vs. competitors

The next table compares characteristics of PFLD and five of the largest preferred stocks ETFs:

  • iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF)
  • First Trust Preferred Securities & Income ETF (FPE)
  • Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX)
  • Global X U.S. Preferred ETF (PFFD)

The next chart plots total returns since PFLD inception. PFLD is in the middle of the pack, significantly lagging the ex-financials ETF PFXF.

All these funds have negative price returns for this period.

PFLD is the worst performer over the last 12 months, but it was less volatile than its peers, as reported below.

Takeaway

AAM Low Duration Preferred and Income Securities ETF holds 209 preferred and hybrid securities with lower sensitivity to interest rate variations. The PFLD portfolio is diversified in many issuers, but very concentrated in the financial sector, especially in banks. PFLD has suffered a significant decay in price since its inception, amplified by inflation. The dividend growth rate has just offset inflation, but the track record is short. ETFs in the same asset category with a longer track record show a long-term decay in both price and distributions. This is not due to strategy or management issues, but an intrinsic issue in high-yield securities. High yields come with high risks, and it’s difficult to make an award-winning fund within a lagging asset class. Investors who absolutely want some exposure to preferred stocks may consider ETFs avoiding financials like PFXF, reviewed here.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Rebounds Despite SEC Filing Delays
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since our last buy rating on Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI), the technical analysis has proven accurate. The stock rebounded 10% from oversold territory following overblown fears from the Hindenburg Research short report. This recovery affirms our view of SMCI’s resilience and potential for significant upside.

SMCI remains strategically positioned in the booming AI infrastructure market, shipping over 100,000 GPUs per quarter, which could generate up to $16 billion in annual revenue from GPU sales alone. Its liquid cooling advancements and energy-efficient systems, such as the H14 powered by AMD's (AMD) EPYC processors, give it a competitive advantage in serving AI-driven data centers requiring high-performance, energy-efficient computing solutions.

While legal uncertainties over delayed financial filings persist, the robust demand for AI infrastructure should continue to drive SMCI’s top-line growth. As the AI sector expands, SMCI’s revenue potential remains strong, likely mitigating the impact of temporary legal pressures and supporting further stock price appreciation.

SMCI Set for Potential 205% Revenue Growth in Q1 2025 Amid Strong AI Infrastructure Momentum

As expected, Super Micro Computer's stock has recovered considerably from a drop in market valuations after Hindenburg Research's short report (published on August 27, 2024). Focusing on the company’s top-line, a material change in 2024 financial figures is less likely. As per SEC Form 12b-25, the company has not confirmed any material changes in reported financials.

Even JPMorgan analysts labeled some of Hindenburg’s claims as “tough to verify.” The proposed submission of Form 10-K 2024 may not have any considerable downside for the company. This may lead to a rapid upside move in the stock price. But there may still be other risks that may cap the stock’s upside, like relative bottom-line underperformance. Overall, the drop in the price since March 2024 is making the stock fairly valued, as the P/S ratio is getting closer to its long-term average with strong top-line projections.

Fundamentally, SMCI’s strategic lead in the AI infrastructure market is based on its shipping capability of over 100K GPUs per quarter. The figure is significant in its direct revenue contribution and alignment with the larger AI demand boom. If one considers the average price of a GPU, say $40K per unit based on Nvidia’s (NVDA) pricing, this translates into $4 billion in potential quarterly revenue solely from GPU shipments. Annually, this equates to a projected $16 billion in revenue from GPU sales alone. Such substantial contributions from high-margin products in a rapidly expanding market indicates solid revenue potential driven by AI demand.

Moreover, the company has delivered over 2K Liquid-Cooled Racks Since June 2024 (as of October 7, 2024), targeting the AI factories market. SMCI’s expansion into liquid-cooled solutions for AI infrastructure further provides its competitive advantage in terms of product differentiation. In short, liquid cooling addresses energy efficiency concerns, making SMCI’s offerings more attractive to clients, as there is an increased focus on heightened energy demands for AI data centers.

For instance, SMCI’s newly launched H14 systems, powered by the 5th generation AMD EPYC processors, deliver 2.44x faster performance than the previous H11 systems. This notable improvement in computational efficiency is critical for AI workloads that require immense processing power and high throughput. The H14 systems utilize 192-core EPYC processors with 500W thermal design power (TDP) that maximizes performance while maintaining energy efficiency. The TDP metric reflects the system’s capability to handle intensive computing tasks while minimizing energy consumption per unit of performance.

Additionally, SMCI’s advancements in liquid cooling technology address the needs of AI-driven data centers, which are experiencing a surge in demand for computing power. The company’s rack-CDU solutions can handle up to 250 kW of cooling capacity with state-of-the-art efficiency, allowing SMCI to cater to data centers that house the latest GPUs and CPUs. This infrastructure lowers clients’ total cost of ownership (TCO) and optimizes energy consumption. By reducing power demand by up to 40%, these solutions allow data centers to fit more servers within a fixed power envelope, increasing their computational capacity without expanding their energy footprint.

Market-wide, there was massive data center investment in the last two years based on growing digitalization and the uptake of AI. This trend may accelerate, as most of the AI Capex spending is concentrated in the US, with China and the European Union having increased AI data center activity. As per IEA - International Energy Agency, the capital investment in 2023 by Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) was larger than that of the US oil and gas industry (~0.5% of US GDP).

SMCI’s energy-efficient products are vital in the market as energy costs are a significant concern. Specifically, AI workloads may grow by 25%-35% per year through 2027. For more data centers, there may be a 30% boost in demand for specific upstream components by 2026. For this, SMCI’s introduction of systems featuring Nvidia's GB200 NVL72 for exascale computing and AMD EPYC 9005 Series CPUs positions SMCI in the AI and high-performance computing (HPC) markets may lead to robust growth in its top-line.

Moreover, Bain & Company estimates that the demand for AI-related hardware and software may increase annually by 40% and 55% by 2027. This provides a supporting indication that SMCI is operating in a rapidly growing space with massive opportunities for top-line expansion.

Finally, The Street revenue estimates align with the above projections. The consensus revenue estimate for fiscal Q1 2025 (ended Sep 2024) is $6.46 billion, with 205% growth. There has been no downside revenue revision during the last three months, with seven upside revisions. Moreover, following the last coverage, the stock has taken support as expected (after the split) and is moving upwards. The targets are unchanged, and the stock may hit an average of $65 ($665 before the split).

SMCI Faces NASDAQ Non-Compliance as Delayed Financials Raise Red Flags: Stock Correction Continues

The delayed filing of SMCI’s 2024 annual report is a significant fundamental weakness, not because of allegations, but because of the consistency of reporting financials. The company has admitted to needing additional time to complete its assessment of internal controls over financials.

Notably, this marks a worrying trend, as the company is about nine weeks behind in reporting its annual report, due in August 2024. In response to this delay, SMCI received a non-compliance notice from NASDAQ on September 17, 2024, threatening its continued listing on the exchange if it fails to comply within 60 to 180 days.

Lastly, the stock may continue to face downward pressure, as the market puts Nvidia and SMCI in the same AI performance category, but there is a massive difference in top-line and bottom-line growth performance. The drop in the stock price since March 2024 is correcting this.

Takeaway

Positioned strongly in AI infrastructure, SMCI’s ability to ship over 100,000 GPUs per quarter supports significant revenue potential, with projected annual GPU sales reaching $16 billion. While delayed filings pose legal uncertainties, AI-driven demand for its energy-efficient systems should continue driving growth, easing market concerns and supporting further stock price appreciation.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Cautious Into Earnings Near All-Time Highs
data.description:
In a little over a week, Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) will report earnings. And by and large, the results should be pretty solid and provide ample reason to continue the strength the stock has shown - at least on its face. But factors beyond reason and logic drive stock prices and the market. While Meta will likely continue to provide decent relative growth, with the stock at all-time highs as it enters not only a slowing period of revenue growth, but an increase in expenses is a reason I'm hesitant to expect more upside.

The Shift In Fundamentals From A High Base

Lapping high sales growth by itself is difficult, but lapping both high sales growth and low expense growth at the same time is an interesting recipe for the market to handle. In August, I outlined this coming challenge and how Q4 will be the first quarter to highlight this. Thus, guidance will be interesting. But since then, the market has rewarded Meta with an expanding margins valuation as the valuation returns to more historically average levels.

Once the 2025 picture comes into focus, it won't be an easy game to play when the massive AI investments start parking themselves on the P&L while revenue growth naturally slows down as it laps high growth from last year and this year. Meta's only out is to open up a new revenue source. Some of its options include increasing ad inventory in Reels or pushing further into the monetization of WhatsApp. Even if it were to decrease AI investments tomorrow - which I don't advocate for (more on that in a bit) - the already built infrastructure of AI accelerators brings more costs and a wave of depreciation.

Because the nature of the business world requires growth to accelerate or, at the very least, be maintained at higher levels to continue to be rewarded, the 2025 revenue outlook and opportunity to find new ample growth opportunities become the primary concern. The chart below, which I used in August's analysis, shows the past two years of high growth and high spending are about to settle in. The obvious piece is the crossover in revenue growth and expense growth, which should begin in earnest in Q4.

Once expense growth exceeds revenue growth, margins decline. While I don't expect a return to 2022 or early 2023 margin levels, they may have peaked for the foreseeable future, setting the stage for challenging bottom line growth in 2025.

Overcoming The Natural Slowdown

Expense growth will remain elevated as depreciation begins to set in from the capex in AI infrastructure over the last two years, along with the operating costs of said infrastructure. While the investments are necessary to keep Meta in the lead amongst its competitors in the advertising space, they come with a natural cost. But if it can become the most efficient player with those purchases, there's no reason it shouldn't continue to make the investments - which it has already planned for 2025. This, of course, is why new forms of revenue or wringing out more growth from current revenue centers are required. Without it, the investments pile on and don't accelerate growth like they did this past year, and margins shrink, choking out profits.

I'm still very much bullish on Meta's AI prospects as it has made the right decisions by open-sourcing its LLM and investing its capabilities in the front and back end of its products - the front end being the user feed and the back end being the ad targeting. The general capabilities of AI at this point lend themselves naturally to Meta's primary business model, much like GPUs lend themselves naturally to training and inferencing generative AI. Other companies like Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) are more shoehorning generative AI into their products as enhancements or new products altogether. Meta is primarily augmenting its products by having AI do the same things but better due to the nature of the algorithms and pattern recognition strengths this type of AI is designed for.

The catch-22 for Meta to remain competitive is it must continue investing in AI and executing as it has, even though the investments increase costs and decrease margins (in the short term) until the investments provide greater operating efficiency (long term).

Sizing Expectations In The Context Of The Stock

Now, much of this isn't terribly new information to you. After all, I covered the financials two months ago. But what has changed is what I alluded to at the beginning of this piece: The stock price.

Lower expectations as the company enters a slowing growth and higher expense period are one thing, but it's another to send the stock to all-time highs. These all-time highs also correlate with an important Fibonacci level, signaling the potential end to a two-year-long rally. This combination is why Meta is more likely to fall after earnings than to continue higher in the wake of the report.

Generally, a five-wave move in Elliot Wave Theory will culminate at or near the 200% extension of waves 1 and 2. With Meta's rally from 2022 now just above the 200% extension and bouncing back below it, the risk of further decline has increased. A setup is in place to see a bounce going into earnings and a drop afterward.

Now, there's an upside risk. Since the correction I ultimately expect will be in the larger hot pink/purple degree, no matter the situation, I'm expecting three waves down. Three waves down could also signal further upside since the rally higher would provide five waves up. So the risk is this is just a smaller-degree three-wave decline before the stock makes another high. The next target for that high is $662 at the next higher Fib level. A post-earnings rally would likely target that.

However, if we see the stock bounce toward $590-$600 by the time earnings arrive, there's a better than 50% chance the C wave down of this initial move off the high is getting prepared to happen on earnings. This targets $562 and below. From there, a three-wave bounce confirms the correction has already started.

Higher Expenses Meets Higher Share Risk

Considering the fundamentals, financials, and the chart, I have to be very cautious going into earnings. Nothing prominent in the earnings report may cause the selloff in a conventional wisdom manner, but it may happen nonetheless. Considering Meta's stock has rallied quite well over the last two years, there isn't anything unusual in a healthy consolidation and correction before the next even bigger leg gets set up for the coming years. It just so happens the timing aligns with an earnings catalyst as it reached the target of the two-year rally.

data.title: Halozyme Therapeutics: Behind The Pullback
data.description:
When we last visited Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:HALO) in early June, I noted I had a large position in the name that was acquired via covered call orders when the stock was in the mid-$30s. While the shares were reasonably valued in the $50s, I was not adding to the position. My game plan was to let my holdings expire in the money and then wait for a decent pullback before looking to do a 'rinse, wash and repeat' covered call trade.

The first step of that plan has been fulfilled as most of my covered call position in Halozyme have been redeemed as the options expired in the money around the holdings. The stock moved up to the mid-$60s briefly in late August but have since pulled back some 20%. Time to establish another position in HALO, or let the stock slide some more before doing so? This is the question I have been asked by a Seeking Alpha follower. Therefore, an updated analysis follows below.

As a reminder, the company utilizes its ENHANZE drug delivery platform. This process enhances the delivery of others approved injected drugs via an advantageous subcutaneous formulation. This improves the infusion times for the reformulated drugs. Halozyme collects milestone payouts and royalties on commercialized sales for its efforts.

The stock started to falter in September after hitting all-time highs in late August as analyst firms started to sour on the equity from a valuation perspective as the shares were up some 70% YTD. On September 19th, JP Morgan bumped up its price target on HALO to $57 a share from $52 previously. However, it cut its rating on the stock from Overweight to Neutral, largely citing valuation for the rating change. On October 7th, Wells Fargo downgraded HALO to Equal Weight ($62 price target) citing limited upside after the stock's YTD outperformance.

Recent Results:

There was little wrong with Halozyme's most recent quarterly results. The company posted its Q2 numbers on August 6th. Halozyme delivered non-GAAP earnings of 91 cents a share, 15 cents a share above expectations. Net income for the quarter was $93.2 million, compared to $74.8 million from the same period a year ago.

Revenues came in at $231 million, some $25 million north of the consensus. Management also reaffirmed its FY2024 from early June and expects profit growth of between 32% to 46% over FY2023 on 13% to 22% sales growth. Guidance was revised up in June based on a patent litigation win in Europe around the company's ENHANZE platform.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Just under eight percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Halozyme ended the first half of 2024 with $529 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. The company listed approximately $1.5 billion in long-term debt on the 10-Q it filed for the second quarter. Halozyme generated a bit over $190 million in operational cash flow in the first two quarters of this year. Management bought back $250 million worth of its own stock in the second quarter and has repurchased $1.3 billion of its own stock over the past five years.

As noted earlier, the analyst community has become mixed on the company's prospect following the large rally in the stock through the end of August. Since second quarter results hit the wires, five analyst firms have downgraded to or maintained Hold ratings on the shares. Five other analyst firms including Morgan Stanley and TD Cowen have reissued Buy ratings with price targets proffered ranging from $60 to $72 a share.

Conclusion:

Halozyme made $2.77 a share on just over $829 million in revenue in FY2023. The current analyst firm consensus has profits moving up sharply in FY2024 to $3.96 a share as sales rise to $894 million. They project $4.74 a share in FY2025 on 16% sales growth. Management believes Halozyme can get to $1 billion in annual royalties/revenues by FY2027.

As I noted in an article earlier this week, I used covered call orders to establish positions throughout the biopharma/biotech space. First, because the SPDR® S&P Biotech ETF (XBI) has been range bound for years and is trading near the exact levels of five years ago. Second, the sector is high beta compared to the overall market. That means option premiums are lucrative, and myriad stocks have good liquidity in those options.

Halozyme is an ideal stock in the sector for a covered call trade in that option premiums around the equity have decent liquidity. In addition, the company produces a consistent and rising revenue stream and is profitable. In short, it is not a clinical stage biotech whose stock can plunge 50% or more in one day on a failed trial result.

The last covered call holdings I have in HALO will expire in the money on November 15th. I will look to reinitiate a small position in HALO at that time if the shares stay at these levels or continue to move lower. The stock is not expensive at 13 times forward earnings given the S&P 500 trades at just over 23 times forward earnings, and HALO is delivering solid and consistent growth in both revenues and profits. Hopefully, by option expiration, there is an overdue pullback in the overall market, and I can acquire my new stake in HALO in the mid to high $40s for another covered call opportunity.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Buy This Bargain Before It's Gone
data.description:
Headquartered in Minnesota, UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is one of the largest companies in the global healthcare services market.

Investment thesis

According to CMS analysis, national health expenditures will continue to grow relentlessly by single-digit percentages in the coming years, reaching $7.17 trillion by 2031, in part due to the aging of the US population, the observed increase in the number of people suffering from cardiovascular disease and various chronic illnesses in the post-COVID-19 era, which in turn will lead to a rise in the number of diagnostic and surgical procedures, as well as prescriptions for drugs that directly affect Optum Rx's revenue.

Yet, due to the company's strong position in the fast-growing health insurance market, not only has it continued to consistently beat Wall Street analysts' expectations in recent quarters, many of whom remain conservative about UnitedHealth Group's business prospects, but more importantly, its revenue and operating income have grown steadily year over year.

As a result, I'm initiating coverage of UnitedHealth Group with a "buy" rating.

UnitedHealth Group's financial results for the third quarter and outlook beyond 2024

On October 15, UnitedHealth Group released its results for the third quarter of 2024, which beat not only my expectations but also Wall Street analysts' forecasts, despite a February 2024 cyberattack on its Change Healthcare unit that caused temporary chaos in the U.S. healthcare system as data transmission between health facility organizations and insurance companies was disrupted.

The company's revenue was $100.82 billion for the three months ended September 30, 2024, up 9.2% year-on-year and, importantly, beating the consensus forecast of $992.8 million.

In my opinion, the key contributor to the growth of this financial metric was its segment called UnitedHealthcare, which provides a wide range of health benefit plans and services to individuals and employers not only in the United States but also abroad through four businesses.

As I always say in my analytical articles about pharmaceutical companies, the numbers reflecting the changes in a company's revenue and net income are undoubtedly important, but I believe the primary focus should be on identifying key factors and assessing the impact of each on the development of the UnitedHealthcare segment in recent months and over the long term.

Like most of you, the first logical question that comes to mind is this.

What factors contributed to the increase in the UnitedHealthcare segment's revenue year-on-year?

First, its total revenue was $74.85 billion for the three months ended September 30, 2024, up 7.2% year-on-year.

The growth of this financial metric was driven by UnitedHealthcare's expansion of its service offerings and improvements in their quality, which ultimately led to an increase in the number of customers served through Medicare Advantage (from 7.65 million people in Q3 2023 to 7.81 million in Q3 2024) and those with commercial health plan products within the U.S. (from 27.25 million to 29.73 million people).

On the other hand, due to the sale of UnitedHealthcare's Brazilian health insurance business to José Seripieri Filho in 2023, the total number of people served globally dropped from 5.48 million to 1.34 million at the end of September 2024, thereby becoming the main reason for the slowdown in the segment's revenue growth rate quarter-on-quarter.

Readers should not forget about the company's second segment, called Optum, which demonstrated higher year-over-year revenue growth.

Total revenue for this segment, including Optum eliminations, reached about $63.79 billion in the third quarter of 2024, an increase of 12.5% year over year and 2% quarter over quarter.

However, what is more important to me is that Optum's revenue shows more stable positive growth dynamics relative to UnitedHealthcare despite the cyberattack on Change Healthcare at the end of February 2024.

What are the reasons behind Optum's successful business development?

Firstly, I would like to note that the successful development of this segment in recent years is directly related to two of its units, namely Optum Health and Optum Insight.

Optum Health's revenue reached $25.92 billion in the third quarter of 2024, up 8.6% year-on-year, driven primarily by the expansion of the types of health-related products it provides, which ultimately translates into an increase in the number of customers served under value-based care arrangements. At the same time, this unit served 104 million people at the end of September 2024, up one million from the previous year.

Meanwhile, Optum Rx's revenue reached $34.21 billion for the three months ended September 30, 2024, up 18.5% year-on-year and 5.5% quarter-on-quarter, mainly due to the expansion of the range of pharmacy services, as well as higher script volumes from new customers. Overall, this unit of Optum fulfilled 410 million adjusted scripts in the third quarter of 2024, which is 30 million more than in the third quarter of 2023.

The Seeking Alpha platform offers financial data on Wall Street analysts' anticipations for the coming quarters.

So, Wall Street analysts expect UnitedHealth Group's revenue for the fourth quarter of 2024 to be between $100.48 billion and $104.14 billion, which implies its growth of 7.8% year over year.

Meanwhile, its price/sales ratio [FWD] is 1.41x, which is not only 3.1% higher than the 5-year average but also higher than many of its competitors in the U.S. health insurance market, including Humana [P/S ratio is 0.29x] (HUM) and Elevance Health [P/S ratio is 0.57x] (ELV), thus being the first of the factors that, in my opinion, indicate that the company is trading at a slight premium at the moment.

On a more global level, UnitedHealth Group's revenue is expected to continue to grow in the coming years, thanks in part to factors such as the increased likelihood of exacerbations of chronic diseases caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, the continued positive growth trend of people covered by its health insurance plans, as well as the aging population, which is driving up healthcare costs in the United States.

So, I anticipate its total revenue to be $552 billion in 2028, which firstly implies its average annual growth over the next five years by single-digit percentages, and secondly, it is in line with the forecasts of two analysts. Accordingly, its P/S ratio will decrease from 1.53x over the past 12 months to 1.04x by 2028.

Moving on to a discussion of an equally important financial indicator, namely non-GAAP earnings per share [non-GAAP EPS], which, in my opinion, more accurately and comprehensively determines how effective the business strategies implemented under the leadership of CEO Andrew Witty are.

So, its non-GAAP EPS for the three months ended September 30, 2024, was $7.15, beating analysts' consensus estimates by 12 cents. In comparison, it is expected to be in the range of $6.45 to $7.03 in the fourth quarter of 2024, implying growth of 10.06% year over year.

Moving to a longer time frame, I expect UnitedHealth Group's trend of increasing operating income to continue despite the temporary reduction in Medicare Advantage funding, including through optimization of general expenses, as well as growth in the number of consumers served through its commercial offerings.

Ultimately, due to the expected commitment to the share buyback program in the coming years, as well as the increase in its revenue under my forecast, which was noted earlier, I expect the company's EPS to be $44 in 2028, which is near the upper limit of the analysts' range [$43.45-$44.08] and also implies a decrease in the P/E ratio from 22.75x to 12.99x.

It is equally important to discuss debt, which, in my opinion, is not a risk to UnitedHealth Group's financial position even given its gradual growth since 2021, since the company's EBITDA continues to grow year-on-year, including due to the factors I indicated in the article earlier, as well as the steps taken by its management aimed at optimizing administrative expenses.

The growth rate of its net debt/EBITDA ratio is not that high and, more importantly, remains below 1.5x at the end of September 2024. As a result, I believe this suggests no considerable financial risks to UnitedHealth Group's financial position.

I would also like to point out that UnitedHealth Group's credit ratings from S&P Global Ratings (SPGI), Fitch, and Moody's (MCO) remain at investment grade.

Risks

I would like to note the following risks that may negatively affect UnitedHealth Group's investment attractiveness.

Takeaway

The company strives to improve access and efficiency of the healthcare system through its two main divisions, Optum and UnitedHealthcare, with the primary goal of improving the quality of life for millions of employees of large corporations and individuals.

The company's revenue growth and EBIT margin, its high return on equity relative to its key competitors, including CVS Health (CVS) and Humana, double-digit growth in its dividend payments year-on-year, and the predictability of its business model make UnitedHealth Group an extremely attractive stock for long-term investors, especially after the sharp drop in its share price caused by a slight decrease in its 2024 adjusted EPS guidance.

data.title: Gold Technical: Bullish Acceleration In Progress Reinforced By 'Trump Trade'
data.description:
This is a follow-up analysis of our prior report “Gold Technical: Poised for a potential bullish breakout as US CPI looms” published on 11 September 2024.

Since our last publication, the price actions of Gold (XAU/USD) have staged a bullish breakout and cleared above the US$2,640/715 medium-term resistance. The yellow metal has rallied by 9.6% from 11 September to Monday, 21 October current all-time high of US$2,740.

“Trump Trade” narrative has reinforced the uptrend in Gold

The “Trump Trade” narrative has gained traction in the recent week due to rising odds of Trump winning the US election based on data from betting markets (60%Trump versus 39% Harris based on Real Clear Politics data as of 20 October).

Given that Trump’s “generous” corporate tax cuts proposal to reduce the tax rate to 15% from 21% will likely widen the US federal deficit further, in turn leading the market to question the credit standing of the US government (such as the prospect of more frequent government shutdowns) that may see an erosion of confidence in US Treasuries and strengthened Gold (XAU/USD).

Gold is being used as tail-risk hedge

Trump’s proposed tax and trade tariffs policies are likely to reignite upward inflationary pressures in the medium to long-term.

In addition, geopolitical risk premium has not been totally eradicated yet in the Middle East due to the ongoing Israel-Hamas war.

Hence, higher inflationary pressure and an increase in geopolitical risk premium are deadly concoctions that may lead to stagflation which in turn can spark a potential risk-off episode in the global financial markets.

In the lens of technical analysis, the ratio chart of S&P 500 over Gold (XAU/USD) together with its monthly RSI momentum indictor of the S&P 500 / Gold (XAU/USD) ratio has displayed a significant underperformance of S&P 500 against Gold (XAU/USD) since February 2024 (see Fig 1).

Similar observation has been detected in the past during the peak of the Dot.com bubble in August 2000 before the S&P 500 staged a major correction of 35% over the next two years.

Therefore, the recent heightened demand for Gold (XAU/USD) is likely reinforced by portfolio tail-risk hedging activities as well.

Medium-term uptrend remains intact

The price actions of Gold (XAU/USD) have been trading firmly above its rising 20-day and 50-day moving averages since 9 August 2024 supported by a parallel ascending trendline support seen in the daily RSI momentum indicator (see Fig 2).

These observations suggest that medium-term upside momentum remains intact for Gold (XAU/USD).

Watch the US$2,590 key medium-term pivotal support for the potential continuation of the impulsive up move sequence for the next medium-term resistances to come in at US$2,850/886 and US$2,933 (also the upper boundary of the medium-term ascending channel from 15 February 2024 low).

On the flipside, failure to hold at US$2,590 negates the bullish tone for a multi-week correction sequence to unfold within its major uptrend phase to expose the next medium-term supports at US$2,484 and US$2,360 (also the 200-day moving average).

Original Post
